Based on the gathered evidence, I would still choose \boxed{B}. Here’s why:
While the reference material provides detailed explanations of achondroplasia and its inheritance mechanism, key points reinforce the probability calculations made before. Specifically, since achondroplasia primarily arises from either a parent passing on a pre-existing mutant allele or arising spontaneously, we consider direct transmission probabilities between carriers. If both parents carry one flawed gene (recessive mode allowing expression), there remains exactly a 25% chance of giving birth to an unaffected child – those remaining being 50% for an affected child showing achondroplasia traits plus again 25%, albeit rarely seen, for homozygosity lethal scenarios not surviving infancy given mentioned low survival rates among pure double mutants. Hence, despite nuanced specifics around familial risks discussed elsewhere, core percentages hold true sticking firmly to classical Mendelian genetics principles explaining variable outcomes observed frequently across populations studied systematically scientifically reliably.
